note: Data model for the standalone comparative-theology site. Two layers: (1) a doctrine matrix at the confessional-FAMILY level, and (2) a finer set of traditions/movements each carrying coordinates on several independent axes, so any single spectrum or any 2-axis map can be drawn from the same data. Each family/tradition is represented from its own authoritative voice. Positions are 0-100 on each axis (0 = left label, 100 = right label). These are v1 placements for Woods to refine.

axes.label: extent of the atonement
axes.note: This axis asks for whom Christ died. The 'limited / particular' pole holds that his death was intended to secure the elect; the 'unlimited' pole holds that he died for everyone, though only those who believe receive its benefit. A middle position — 'sufficient for all, efficient for the elect' (Amyraldianism) — holds both at once: the death is sufficient for all, and savingly applied to those who come to faith.

axes.label: baptism
axes.note: The infant-baptizing traditions also baptize adults who come to faith, on their profession — so the left pole marks that infants are included, not that believers are excluded. The right pole is the position that baptizes professing believers only, and never infants.

axes.label: the Spirit’s gifts
axes.note: These are the miraculous 'sign' gifts — speaking in tongues, prophecy, and healing (1 Corinthians 12–14) — not spiritual gifts in general like teaching or mercy, which every tradition affirms. The 'ceased' pole holds that the sign gifts belonged to the apostolic age and are no longer given now that the Church is founded and Scripture complete; the 'for today' pole holds that they continue in the church's life. The middle, 'open but cautious,' neither seeks them out nor rules them out.

Orthodox: The Orthodox Church / The Orthodox Way (Kallistos Ware)
the Nicene faith and the Fathers
Baptist: the 1689 London Baptist Confession for the Reformed side
Pentecostal: Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths

cells.Catholic: Scripture and Tradition together, interpreted by the Magisterium
cells.Orthodox: Scripture within Holy Tradition, read with the mind of the Church
cells.Anglican: Scripture supreme, read alongside tradition and reason
cells.Lutheran: sola scriptura; the norm of norms, creeds subordinate
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: Scripture primary, read with tradition, reason, and experience
cells.Reformed: sola scriptura; Scripture the final judge over all tradition
cells.Baptist: sola scriptura; strong biblicism and individual soul competency
cells.Pentecostal: sola scriptura, with a lively expectation of the Spirit still speaking

cells.Catholic: wounded by sin but able to cooperate with grace
cells.Orthodox: ancestral sin wounds the will but does not destroy its freedom
cells.Anglican: the Articles affirm depravity; prevenient grace restores response
cells.Lutheran: total depravity; the will is bound until regeneration
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: depravity is real, but prevenient grace frees the will to respond
cells.Reformed: total depravity; the will is in bondage
cells.Baptist: varies: general Baptists free, Reformed Baptists bound
cells.Pentecostal: free to respond; conversion is a genuine decision

cells.Catholic: infused and cooperated with, mediated through the sacraments
cells.Orthodox: synergy: grace (God's uncreated energies) and freedom cooperate
cells.Anglican: prevenient grace enables; the tradition holds a range
cells.Lutheran: given through the means of grace, and resistible
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: prevenient and resistible; grace goes before and enables all
cells.Reformed: irresistible and effectual, given to the elect
cells.Pentecostal: resistible; the Spirit empowers, often with Wesleyan-holiness roots

cells.Catholic: predestination held together with real human freedom
cells.Orthodox: grounded in God's foreknowledge, never predestination to damnation
cells.Anglican: Article XVII affirms predestination to life (election), not to damnation; read variously
cells.Lutheran: unconditional election to salvation only, not to damnation
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: conditional election in view of foreseen faith; whosoever will
cells.Reformed: unconditional election; strict Calvinism adds double predestination
cells.Pentecostal: conditional; the offer is to whosoever

detail.Catholic: The Church holds that God wills the salvation of all and, in eternity, predestines the elect to glory — yet never in a way that overrides human freedom or predestines anyone to sin or damnation. Predestination is read inside the mystery of grace: God's initiative is total, and the person still truly cooperates. Thomists and Molinists dispute how grace and freedom fit together, but both keep the two held together rather than sacrificing either.
detail.Orthodox: The East frames election through God's foreknowledge rather than an eternal decree that fixes each person's destiny in advance. Standing outside time, God knows how each person will freely respond, and elects in accord with that foreseen response. There is no predestination to damnation: God desires everyone to be saved, and the person genuinely shares in salvation through the synergy of grace and freedom.
detail.Anglican: Article XVII affirms predestination to life — God's eternal purpose to bring the elect to salvation in Christ — but says nothing of predestination to damnation, leaving reprobation an open question. Because the Article can be read in a Calvinist, Arminian, or Lutheran key, Anglicanism has carried all three readings within one communion. It also cautions pastorally that the doctrine becomes a snare when pressed further than Scripture plainly warrants.
detail.Lutheran: Lutherans confess election to salvation as God's unconditional gift, grounded wholly in Christ and not in any merit or faith foreseen in the person. But they refuse the symmetrical step of teaching predestination to damnation: the lost are lost by their own rejection of grace, not by a decree against them. This 'single predestination' holds God's sovereignty and human responsibility together without pretending to resolve the tension.
detail.Wesleyan/Methodist: Wesley affirmed predestination but read it as conditional: God elects in view of the faith he foreknows, so the invitation is genuinely open to whosoever will. Prevenient grace goes before every person, restoring the freedom to accept or resist, so no one is shut out by an eternal decree. Election, in this account, is God's gracious foreknowledge meeting a real human response — not an arbitrary choice of some over others.
detail.Reformed: In the Reformed confession, election is unconditional: before the foundation of the world God freely chose those he would save, resting salvation entirely on his sovereign grace rather than on foreseen faith. Strict Calvinism presses this to its symmetrical conclusion — double predestination, in which God also ordains the reprobate to their end — while others affirm election alone and leave the rest to God's justice. Either way, the emphasis falls on grace that owes nothing to the creature.
detail.Baptist: The Baptist family is genuinely split here, which is why the matrix cell reads 'varies.' General (Arminian) Baptists hold conditional election — God chooses those he foreknows will believe — while Particular (Reformed) Baptists confess unconditional election in the Calvinist line. Most Baptist churches fall somewhere along that range, and the question of Calvinism remains one of the family's most active internal debates.
detail.Pentecostal: Pentecostals overwhelmingly hold conditional election: the gospel offer is to whosoever will, and the Spirit draws all who are willing to respond. Rooted largely in the Wesleyan-holiness stream, the movement stresses a genuine human decision and the real openness of salvation to anyone, over any decree that would settle the matter apart from a person's response.

cells.Catholic: sufficient for all, applied through the Church's means
cells.Orthodox: for all; framed less as payment than as Christ the victor and healer
cells.Anglican: sufficient for all; the tradition holds a range
cells.Lutheran: universal in scope, received through faith
cells.Reformed: limited / particular: Christ died for the elect
cells.Pentecostal: unlimited; often includes healing in the atonement

cells.Catholic: by grace through faith working in love; righteousness infused, not only declared
cells.Orthodox: framed as healing and participation more than a courtroom verdict
cells.Anglican: by faith (Article XI), rooted in the Reformation
cells.Lutheran: by faith alone; imputed righteousness, its central article
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: by faith alone, but joined to real inward change
cells.Baptist: by faith alone; forensic and imputed
cells.Pentecostal: by faith; a genuine conversion and new birth

cells.Catholic: growth in infused grace, aided by the sacraments
cells.Orthodox: theosis: being made, by grace, a partaker of the divine nature
cells.Anglican: growth in grace through word and sacrament
cells.Lutheran: simul justus et peccator; growth by returning to baptism
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: central; entire sanctification is possible — 'Christian perfection' meaning perfect love, not sinlessness
cells.Reformed: gradual; never completed in this life
cells.Baptist: gradual growth in the Christian life
cells.Pentecostal: holiness plus Spirit baptism as empowerment; sometimes a second work

cells.Catholic: yes: grace can be lost by mortal sin and restored through penance
cells.Orthodox: salvation is an ongoing synergy; one can turn away
cells.Anglican: the Articles do not require eternal security; a range is held
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: yes: salvation can be forfeited; assurance by the Spirit's witness
cells.Reformed: no: the elect persevere and cannot finally fall
cells.Baptist: commonly no: once saved, always saved (eternal security)
cells.Pentecostal: commonly yes: one can fall away (Arminian majority)

cells.Catholic: transubstantiation: the elements become Christ's body and blood
cells.Orthodox: a true mystery: the real presence affirmed, the how left unexplained
cells.Anglican: real spiritual presence; the tradition ranges from memorial to high
cells.Lutheran: real presence in, with, and under the elements (sacramental union)
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: spiritual real presence; a means of grace at an open table
cells.Reformed: spiritual real presence, received by faith
cells.Baptist: a memorial; the elements are symbols (an ordinance)
cells.Pentecostal: an ordinance; the elements are symbolic

cells.Catholic: a regenerating sacrament; infants are baptized
cells.Orthodox: a regenerating mystery; infants baptized and chrismated
cells.Anglican: a regenerating sign; infants are baptized
cells.Lutheran: a regenerating means of grace; infants are baptized
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: a means of grace; infants baptized, but not automatic
cells.Reformed: a covenant sign; infants are baptized
cells.Baptist: believer's baptism only; a symbol, by immersion
cells.Pentecostal: believer's baptism, plus a distinct baptism in the Spirit

cells.Catholic: episcopal and hierarchical: bishops in apostolic succession, in communion with the Pope
cells.Orthodox: bishops in apostolic succession; a communion of self-governing churches, led conciliarly
cells.Anglican: episcopal: bishops, priests, and deacons, with governance shared through synods
cells.Lutheran: varies: some churches keep bishops, others are led by synods and elected presidents
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: connectional: bishops and annual conferences appoint clergy and order the church's life
cells.Reformed: presbyterian: rule by elders through graded courts — session, presbytery, assembly
cells.Baptist: congregational: each local church is self-governing and autonomous under Christ
cells.Pentecostal: varies: from congregational to overseer-led, often with district and general councils

cells.Catholic: ordination is reserved to men; women serve widely but are not ordained priests
cells.Orthodox: the priesthood is male; women serve in monastic, teaching, and historic diaconal roles
cells.Anglican: varies: the Episcopal Church ordains women as priests and bishops; some Anglican bodies do not
cells.Lutheran: varies: the ELCA ordains women; the LCMS reserves the pastoral office to men
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: women are ordained to every office, including bishop, with full clergy rights
cells.Reformed: varies: the PCUSA ordains women; the PCA and confessional bodies ordain only men
cells.Baptist: varies: many Baptists ordain women; the SBC reserves the office of pastor for men
cells.Pentecostal: often open to women preaching and ordination, a mark since its earliest days

cells.Catholic: the charisms are real and continue, exercised under the Church's discernment
cells.Orthodox: the gifts continue, seen especially in the saints, elders, and the Church's life
cells.Anglican: a range, from cautious to the charismatic renewal welcomed in parts of the Communion
cells.Lutheran: the Spirit works through Word and sacrament; the sign gifts are held with caution
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: open to the gifts, with love and holiness of life as their measure
cells.Reformed: commonly cessationist: the sign gifts authenticated the apostles and have ceased
cells.Baptist: commonly cessationist, though many are open but cautious about the sign gifts
cells.Pentecostal: the gifts are for today; tongues, prophecy, and healing mark the Spirit-filled life

cells.Catholic: one salvation history; the Church as the People of God, Israel's covenant never revoked
cells.Orthodox: one divine economy unfolding to Christ and fulfilled in the Church, read through the Fathers
cells.Anglican: one covenant of grace across both Testaments; the Church continuous with Israel
cells.Lutheran: law and gospel run through every age; one people saved by the promise in Christ
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: one unfolding story of grace; the Church grafted into the covenant made with Israel
cells.Reformed: covenant theology: one covenant of grace administered differently through the ages
cells.Baptist: varies: from covenantal to dispensational, some sharply distinguishing Israel and the Church
cells.Pentecostal: often premillennial, expecting Christ's soon return; many hold a dispensational frame

cells.Catholic: the Mass at the center: liturgical, sacramental, ordered by the Church's calendar
cells.Orthodox: the Divine Liturgy: ancient, sung, iconographic, and unchanging in shape
cells.Anglican: ordered by the Book of Common Prayer, ranging from high ceremonial to low
cells.Lutheran: liturgical and hymn-rich, centered on the preached Word and the Sacrament
cells.Wesleyan/Methodist: a blend: ordered liturgy joined to revival warmth and Wesley's hymnody
cells.Reformed: Word-centered and orderly, historically plain, with the sermon at the center
cells.Baptist: free and informal, the sermon central; style ranges from traditional to contemporary
cells.Pentecostal: expressive and spontaneous: extended praise, testimony, and freedom for the Spirit

traditions.name: Eastern Orthodox
traditions.blurb: Patristic and liturgical; mystery, the Mysteries, and theosis. Practice sits beside Rome, but its participatory, apophatic spirit reaches toward Wesley.

traditions.name: Roman Catholic
traditions.blurb: Church, Tradition, and Magisterium together; seven sacraments; transubstantiation; grace mediated through the sacramental life.

traditions.name: Anglo-Catholic
traditions.blurb: The sacramental, apostolic wing of Anglicanism; high liturgy and Prayer Book piety.

traditions.name: Lutheran (LCMS)
traditions.blurb: Confessional Lutheranism; sola scriptura plus real presence, strongly liturgical and confessional.

traditions.name: Lutheran (ELCA)
traditions.blurb: Mainline Lutheranism; the same sacramental core as the LCMS, the mainline of the two major US Lutheran bodies.

traditions.name: Episcopal (US)
traditions.blurb: The US expression of Anglicanism; deeply sacramental and broadly inclusive.

traditions.name: Methodist / Wesleyan (UMC/GMC)
traditions.blurb: The via media: means of grace joined to revival warmth. Center of the field, which is why it can bridge in several directions.

traditions.name: Presbyterian (PCUSA)
traditions.blurb: Mainline Reformed; shares the Westminster heritage of the PCA, in the mainline branch.

traditions.name: Anabaptist / Mennonite
traditions.blurb: The believers' church: discipleship, peace, and separation from the world; believer's baptism.
traditions.card_denoms: the Amish, the Brethren, the Hutterites, and the Bruderhof

traditions.name: Restorationist (Churches of Christ)
traditions.blurb: No creed but the Bible; believer's baptism, often tied to regeneration; congregational.
traditions.card_denoms: the Christian Churches / Churches of Christ and the Disciples of Christ

traditions.name: Pentecostal / charismatic (AoG)
traditions.blurb: Spirit baptism and the gifts; experiential and low-sacrament, with Wesleyan-holiness roots.

traditions.name: New Calvinist / Reformed evangelical
traditions.blurb: Acts 29, the Gospel Coalition, Piper, Keller: TULIP soteriology in a largely baptistic, Bible-expositional key.

traditions.name: Mainstream / seeker evangelical
traditions.blurb: Saddleback, Willow Creek: conversionist, contemporary, light on doctrine by design.
traditions.card_denoms: the Willow Creek and Saddleback networks and other seeker-model community churches

traditions.name: Nondenominational megachurch
traditions.blurb: Highlands, North Point, Elevation, 3 Circle: a baptist, reformed, and charismatic amalgam; the fastest-growing space.
traditions.card_denoms: Acts 29, the ARC network, and independent nondenominational megachurches

traditions.name: General / Free Will Baptist
traditions.blurb: Conversionist, believer's baptism, non-Calvinist, resistible grace, salvation can be lost.
traditions.card_denoms: General Baptists and other free-will (Arminian) Baptist bodies

traditions.name: Southern Baptist
traditions.blurb: The biblicist pole: Scripture alone, believer's baptism, symbolic ordinances, conversion-centered.
traditions.card_denoms: the National and Progressive National Baptist Conventions, American Baptist Churches USA, and independent Baptist churches

traditions.name: Independent Fundamental / fundamentalist
traditions.blurb: Biblicist and separatist; the hard edge of the pole, often dispensational.
traditions.card_denoms: independent fundamental Baptist (IFB) churches and separatist Bible churches

traditions.name: UCC / Unitarian
traditions.blurb: The tradition descended from New England Congregationalism; non-creedal and congregational, holding the broadest doctrinal latitude of the bodies shown here.

voices_note: Authors and leaders for each tradition, listed only where the person identifies (or identified) with that tradition, not merely sits near it theologically. Groups run founders/classic figures, contemporary voices, popular authors ('On the shelf today'), and living 'Current Notable Figures'. Women and persons of color included wherever the tradition has them. Illustrative, not exhaustive; for Woods to refine.

orthodox.note: Read with the mind of the Church; the Fathers hold pride of place.

lcms.note: Confessional Lutheranism; the Book of Concord held without reservation. Shares its root — Luther, Melanchthon, and Bonhoeffer — with the ELCA, but diverges sharply on Scripture, women's ordination, and ecumenism.

groups.label: Founders & confessors

Philip Melanchthon — the Augsburg Confession
Martin Chemnitz — second Martin of the Confession
C.F.W. Walther — founder of the Missouri Synod

elca.note: Mainline Lutheranism; the same root as the LCMS — Luther, Melanchthon, Bonhoeffer — in a body that ordains women and LGBTQ clergy and reads Scripture critically.

free-will-baptist.note: Conversionist, believer's baptism, non-Calvinist; grace is resistible and salvation can be lost.

groups.label: Founders
people: Thomas Helwys — the first General Baptist congregation
Benjamin Randall — the Free Will Baptist movement in New England
Paul Palmer — early Free Will Baptist churches in the South

groups.label: Scholars
people: F. Leroy Forlines — The Quest for Truth
Robert Picirilli — Grace, Faith, Free Will

sbc.note: Scripture alone, believer's baptism, symbolic ordinances, conversion-centered.

groups.label: Founders & shapers
people: E.Y. Mullins — soul competency
Lottie Moon — missionary and namesake of the missions offering
Annie Armstrong — missions organizer
George W. Truett — Dallas pastor

Fred Luter — first African-American SBC president
David Platt — Radical
Al Mohler — Southern Seminary

fundamentalist.note: Biblicist and separatist; the hard edge of the pole, often dispensational. Leadership is historically narrow, so this is mostly its founders.

groups.label: Founders & shapers
people: C.I. Scofield — the Scofield Reference Bible
R.A. Torrey — editor of The Fundamentals
William Bell Riley — fundamentalist organizer
Bob Jones Sr. — evangelist and college founder
John R. Rice — The Sword of the Lord

ucc-unitarian.note: The end of the Protestant spectrum descended from Congregationalism; known for a series of 'firsts.'

groups.label: Historic firsts
people: Antoinette Brown Blackwell — first woman ordained in an American denomination
Lemuel Haynes — first African-American ordained by a Protestant body in America
Horace Bushnell — Christian Nurture

groups.label: Later voices
people: Washington Gladden — the Social Gospel
William Ellery Channing — Unitarian founder
Yvonne Delk — African-American UCC minister and activist

groups.label: On the shelf today
people: Otis Moss III — Dancing in the Darkness (Trinity UCC, Chicago)

groups.label: Current Notable Figures
people: Otis Moss III — pastor of Trinity UCC and author
Traci Blackmon — UCC minister and justice leader
John Dorhauer — former UCC General Minister and President

authority.note: Our own take on Donald Durnbaugh's 'Typology of Churches': each tradition placed by where it locates authority — the Word (Scripture and preaching), the Spirit (experience and revelation), or Tradition (the church and its sacraments). v1 placements for Woods to refine.

other_voices.name: Rob Bell
other_voices.professed: nondenominational, then left the local church

other_voices.name: Francis Chan
other_voices.professed: nondenominational; left his megachurch for house churches

family_denominations.Catholic: the Roman Catholic Church and the Eastern-rite Catholic churches (Maronite, Melkite, Ukrainian Greek Catholic)
family_denominations.Orthodox: the Eastern Orthodox churches (Greek, Russian, Antiochian, OCA) and the Oriental Orthodox churches (Armenian Apostolic, Coptic, Ethiopian, Syriac)
family_denominations.Anglican: the Episcopal Church, the Anglican Church in North America (ACNA), and the Church of England and wider Anglican Communion; at the edge, the United Church of Christ
family_denominations.Lutheran: the ELCA, the Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod (LCMS), the Wisconsin Synod (WELS), and the North American Lutheran Church
family_denominations.Wesleyan/Methodist: the United Methodist Church and the Global Methodist Church; the AME, AME Zion, and CME churches; the Church of the Nazarene, the Free Methodist Church, the Wesleyan Church, and the Salvation Army
family_denominations.Reformed: the PCUSA, the PCA, the EPC and OPC; the Reformed Church in America and the Christian Reformed Church; and the Congregational churches
family_denominations.Baptist: the Southern Baptist Convention, the National and Progressive National Baptist Conventions, American Baptist Churches USA, and the Free Will Baptists; the Churches of Christ; Mennonite and other Anabaptist churches; and many nondenominational and community churches
family_denominations.Pentecostal: the Assemblies of God, the Church of God in Christ (COGIC), the Church of God (Cleveland, TN), the Foursquare Church, and the wider charismatic movement
